---
title: "EEG-ECMR Progress"
author: "Jordan Gunn"
date: last-modified
number-sections: true
format: html
---

The goal of this document is to document progress and note key decisions made during the development of a neurocognitive extension of eCMR using EEG data from Zarubin et al. (2020).

In the current project phase, the primary focus is on developing a simple prototype that demonstrates the feasibility of using item-level LPP signals to enhance eCMR's predictions of the emotion-enhanced memory effect.

Future phases may explore alternative model specifications, fitting procedures, and evaluation metrics.

# Data Preparation

Initially I prepared `Single_Trial_Behavioural_and_EEG_Data_Z.csv` from the lab drive, but missing study events and retrieval-success fields limited its value for model evaluation.
I combined that partial LPP dataset with the more complete behavioral file `All_Included_Subjects.csv`.
The file lacks Early/Late LPP scores but supplies full study-event records.
For study events without LPP data, I imputed missing values using within-subject, within-trial averages for items in the same emotional category.
Finally, I verified the completeness of this compiled dataset: 9 trials per participant, 20 study events per trial, each with a recall outcome.

It's important to highlight that participants actually studied 22 items per trials, but the first two items from each trial were excluded from analysis to mitigate primacy effects.
This is regrettable for model evaluation since CMR-style models often rely on early list items to establish context dynamics.
Nonetheless, we will carefully navigate this limitation in the final version of our analyses.

Even more critically, the dataset does not record the order in which participants recalled items, only whether each item was recalled or not.
This limitation requires custom-designed loss functions for model fitting even at the prototyping stage.

# Benchmark Analyses

Benchmark analyses serve two purposes: validating data preparation and defining concrete targets for model development.
Across plots, error bars reflect between-subjects 95%-bootstrapped confidence intervals unless otherwise noted.

## Recall Rate by Study Position and Item-Condition / Category-SPC

Our core behavioral analysis to address it on a categorized-serial position curve (category-SPC), plotted cleanly in the “Meeting with Nathaniel Daw” slides on the lab drive.
The analysis involves taking the ratio of recalled to total items matching the category label and studied at each serial position.

::: {#fig-reference_catspc layout-ncol="2"}
![](results/figures/daw_meeting_slide5.png)

![](results/figures/reference_catspc.png)

Recall rate by study position and item type from reference materials (LEFT) and as generated from the present dataset (RIGHT).

:::

<!-- TODO: Switch to a hierarchical confidence interval over subjects and trials. Hmm, this might not be as necessary as I thought it would be. -->

The match between shared and generated categorized serial position curves confirms the integrity of data preprocessing.

## LPP by Study Position and Item-Condition

I drew these plots to help interpret the relationship between LPP and serial position / emotionality.
Most approaches we're considering for augmenting eCMR with LPP data will make variation across factors of the category-SPC look more like variation across factors of this plot.

::: {#fig-cat_lpp_spc layout-ncol="2"}
![](results/figures/cat_lpp_spc_EarlyLPP.png)

![](results/figures/cat_lpp_spc_LateLPP.png)

LPP amplitude by study position and item type for Early LPP (LEFT) and Late LPP (RIGHT).
:::

These plots also confirm that both early and late LPP signals vary with item emotionality across study positions.

## Recall Rate by LPP and Item-Condition

A successful neurally-informed extension of eCMR should not just identify emotional and neutral items, but provide additional information beyond these labels about how and whether emotional and neutral items will be recalled.
We thus plot the relationship between LPP values and recall rate across item categories.

::: {#fig-cat_recall_by_early_lpp layout-ncol="2"}
![](results/figures/reference_cat_recall_by_lpp.png)

![](results/figures/cat_recall_by_lpp_EarlyLPP.png)

Recall rate by early LPP amplitude and item type as plotted in reference materials (LEFT) and as generated from the present dataset (RIGHT).
:::

::: {#fig-cat_recall_by_late_lpp}
![](results/figures/cat_recall_by_lpp_LateLPP.png){width="50%"}

Recall rate by Late LPP amplitude and item type.
:::

::: {#fig- layout-ncol="2"}
![](results/figures/cat_recall_by_lpp_EarlyLPP_counts.png)

![](results/figures/cat_recall_by_lpp_LateLPP_counts.png)

Counts of observations per bin for Early LPP (LEFT) and Late LPP (RIGHT) analyses.
:::

::: {#fig-amplitude_by_cat_by_recalled}
![](results/figures/amplitude_by_cat_by_recalled.png)

LPP by time, recall status, and item type.
:::

We observe that there are few observations at high (above 4.86) and low (below -3.25) LPP bins in our version of the analysis.
In an early version of this analysis, I found that the relationship between LPP and recall rate was nonlinear.
In fact, I was accidentally assigning a value 0 to subject-level bin value measurements when no observations were found in that bin.
This led to the underestimation of recall rates in bins with few observations.
With this issue corrected, a relatively positive trend can be observed between LPP values and recall rate, though variability in observation count across bins produces wide confidence intervals at high LPP amplitudes.
An updated version of the analysis may seek to define bins so that the number of observations per bin is more balanced.
Or we might just plot variables a different way.

<!-- TODO: Balance the number of observations per bin by refactoring implementation of procedure from Morton & Polyn (2016) -->

## LPP by Recall Status and Item-Category
To further validate the relationship between LPP and recall outcomes, I plotted LPP amplitudes separately for recalled and unrecalled items within each emotional category.
Since we avoid binning LPP values in this analysis, this approach provides a cleaner view of how LPP varies with recall status across item types.

::: {#fig-cat_lpp_by_recall layout-ncol="2"}

![](results/figures/cat_lpp_by_recall_EarlyLPP_Negative.png)

![](results/figures/cat_lpp_by_recall_EarlyLPP_Neutral.png)

![](results/figures/cat_lpp_by_recall_LateLPP_Negative.png)

![](results/figures/cat_lpp_by_recall_LateLPP_Neutral.png)

LPP amplitude by recall status and item type for Early LPP (TOP row) and Late LPP (BOTTOM row), split by NEGATIVE items (LEFT column) and NEUTRAL items (RIGHT column).
:::

These plots broadly confirm that higher LPP amplitudes are associated with recalled items, but specifically for NEGATIVE items, with no clear relationship for NEUTRAL items. 
The pattern is also much clearer for Early LPP than Late LPP, supporting our focus on Early LPP for neurally-informed model extensions.

# Loss Functions For Model Fitting

We face two main challenges when defining loss functions for fitting memory search models to the present dataset.
First, memory search models like CMR and eCMR typically generate predictions about the order in which items are recalled, but the dataset only records whether each item was recalled or not.
This limitation forces us to focus on a simpler question: **does the composition of recalled items match what the model predicts**?

Second, the first two studied items from each study list were excluded from the prepared dataset. This omission was intended to keep primacy effects from impacting analyses of the emotion-enhanced memory effect, but for fitting memory search models, it removes important information about how study lists were encoded and retrieved. 

These two issues together mean that we cannot use standard likelihood-based loss functions that evaluate the probability of observed recall sequences under the model.
For now, we ignore the excluded items, focusing on fitting models to predict the set of recalled and unrecalled items from each trial which we do have data for.
To simplify model evaluation further, we also exclude evaluation of how models predict the total number of recalled items per trial.
Neither CMR nor eCMR makes strong predictions about recall counts and addressing them requires additional parameters and mechanisms controlling recall termination.
By excluding recall counts as a factor in loss functions and model evaluation, we reduce the complexity of the fitting process, focus squarely on neurally-augmented retrieved-context models' ability to capture which items are recalled (rather than how many or in what order), clarifying the interpretability of model comparisons.

## Set Likelihood

Without recall order data, we must evaluate models based on the probability they assign to the **set** of recalled items in each trial.
The challenge is that memory search models are designed to generate predictions about the order of recalls, and multiple recall sequences are compatible with any given set of recalled items.

The most formally exact approach to overcome this challenge is to enumerate all possible recall sequences consistent with the observed set of recalled items and compute the probability of each sequence under the model.
The probability of the observed data would then be the **sum of the probabilities assigned to all compatible recall sequences**.
However, the number of possible recall orders increases factorially with the number of recalled items, making this approach computationally infeasible for all but the smallest datasets.

To sidestep this combinatorial explosion, a leading approach is to estimate the set likelihood using Monte Carlo sampling: drawing a constrained sample of recall sequences consistent with the observed recall set and averaging their model probabilities.
This yields an unbiased estimate of the set likelihood but introduces sampling variability.
By increasing the number of sampled sequences, the estimate can be made arbitrarily precise, albeit at the cost of increased computation time.
Because reliable estimation would require a large number of samples per trial, we reserve this a complete examination of the approach for more advanced stages of the project.

<!-- TODO: debug why current set likelihood function doesn't work -->

## Mean Squared Error (MSE) Over Summary Statistics

Given these constraints, we instead adopt a mean squared error (MSE) loss between an observed summary statistic and the same statistic generated by simulated data.
This approach is widely used in evaluation of retrieved-context models, but is especially appropriate here given the limitations of our dataset.

The procedure is as follows:

1.  Compute a summary statistic from the experimental data.
2.  Simulate the model repeatedly using a given parameter set.
3.  Compute the same statistic from the simulated outcomes.
4.  Compute the mean squared difference between the observed and simulated statistics.

This MSE provides an interpretable measure of how well a given parameter set allows the model to reproduce the behavioral pattern of interest.
In principle, if the model with the best-fitting parameters cannot reproduce the observed statistic well, we can conclude that the model is inadequate for explaining the data.
While an MSE-based approach does not yield an unbiased estimate of the true likelihood or a maximum-likelihood estimate of parameters and discards trial-level prediction detail, it offers a practical, scalable, and transparent evaluation metric.

In initial evaluations, I experimented with different summary statistics to guide model fitting.
First, I tried using the overall serial position curve (SPC) as the target statistic.
This approach provides a straightforward test of whether the model captures overall primacy and recency trends across the list, ignoring differences between emotional and neutral items.
However, this approach proved insufficient for evaluating the model's ability to capture the emotion-enhanced memory effect, as it does not differentiate between item types.

We thus settled on using the **category-SPC** as the target statistic for model fitting.
Here, recall probability is computed separately for **negative** and **neutral** items at each study position, allowing the model to be evaluated on its ability to reproduce both serial position effects and the emotion-enhanced memory effect simultaneously.
Therefore, the category-SPC provides a more sensitive and relevant benchmark for assessing the performance of neurally-augmented eCMR models in this context.

# Modeling

As a baseline, we begin evaluation with regular CMR, insensitive to item emotionality or LPP data.
We evaluate this model to confirm that the emotion-enhanced memory effect cannot be captured without additional emotional-attention mechanisms.

Next, we implement a simplified version of eCMR that introduces emotional-attention mechanisms with minimal complexity.
Rather than incorporate all parameters and mechanisms from eCMR, we seek to use only enough details to achieve adequate capture of our behavioral benchmark statistics.

From there, we will implement a neurally-informed extension of eCMR that uses event-level LPP measures as modulators of emotional-attentional strength.
We will have two goals for this model.
First, the model should outperform both baseline CMR and simplified eCMR at capturing the emotion-enhanced memory effect.
Second, the model should adequately capture the relationship between event-level LPP values and recall probability for emotional _and_ neutral items.
Accomplishing these two goals together will produce a baseline neurocognitive model of the emotion-enhanced memory effect that can be further refined in future work using higher-quality datasets and more sophisticated model fitting procedures.

## Simplified Pure-Attention eCMR
As an initial step beyond baseline CMR, we postulate that the emotion-enhanced memory effect arises entirely from differential allocation of attention during encoding, enhancing their context-to-item associations. We label this variant "pure-attention eCMR" to distinguish it from the complete eCMR specification.

The complete eCMR specification includes a separate emotional context and corresponding emotional feature-to-context and context-to-feature memories (eMFC and eMCF), with emotion-enhanced attention primarily modulating learning rates within these memory structures.
They thus predict that emotion-enhanced attention primarily strengthens the association between items and emotion states during encoding.
During recall, when emotional context is reinstated, it preferentially cues emotional items via these strengthened associations.

We exclude these dynamics at this stage, instead allowing emotional-attention to directly scale the strength of temporal context-to-item associations during encoding.
We predict that this extension from baseline CMR alone is sufficient to capture the emotion-enhanced memory effect in our dataset, and will evaluate this prediction against more complex multi-component specifications in subsequent evaluations.

### Parameter Sensitivity Tests
In Pure-Attention eCMR, an `emotion_scale` parameter scales the strength of context-to-item associations during encoding for emotional items.

To confirm that the model's extra parameter can address the emotional category-spc, we fit the base model to the dataset then simulate pure-attention eCMR with different configurations of its `emotion_scale` parameter, plotting the emotional category-spc for each set of simulations.

::: {#fig-attention_emcr_parameter_sensitivity layout-ncol="2"}

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_negative_shift_emotion_scale.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_neutral_shift_emotion_scale.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_spc_shift_emotion_scale.png)

Sensitivity of serial position curve to changes in the emotional-attention scaling parameter in pure-attention eCMR. Left: NEGATIVE items. Right: NEUTRAL items. Bottom: ALL items.
:::

These results show that scaling up the parameter increases recall rates for negative items while reducing recall rates for neutral items, building confidence that the parameter can improve the baseline model's ability to address the emotion-enhanced memory effect.
However, specific assumptions made by the model about issues like the interaction between primacy and arousal/emotional-attention as well as latent assumptions about effects on response order or initiation could still throw off model fitting.

### Comparison of Fits to Benchmark Statistics

#### Negative + Neutral Category SPC

::: {#fig-attention_emcr_cat_spc_comparison layout-ncol="2"}

![](results/figures/reference_catspc.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc.png)

Recall rate by study position and item type from reference materials (LEFT) and Pure-Attention eCMR simulation (RIGHT).
:::

::: {#fig-baseline_mcr_cat_spc}

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_WeirdCMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc.png){width="50%"}

Recall rate by study position and item type for Baseline CMR.
:::

#### Single-Category SPC

::: {#fig-attention_emcr_negative_cat_spc layout-ncol="2"}

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_negative.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_WeirdCMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_negative.png)


Recall rate by study position for NEGATIVE items. Left column: Pure-Attention eCMR. Right column: Baseline CMR.
:::

::: {#fig-attention_emcr_neutral_cat_spc layout-ncol="2"}
![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_neutral.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_WeirdCMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_neutral.png)

Recall rate by study position for NEUTRAL items. Left column: Pure-Attention eCMR. Right column: Baseline CMR.
:::

#### LPP by Recall Status and Item-Category

It's also possible to examine how well pure-attention eCMR captures the relationship between LPP amplitude and recall probability for emotional and neutral items.
This sets a higher bar for model performance, since the model must not only capture the emotion-enhanced memory effect but also the graded relationship between LPP and recall probability within each item category.

::: {#fig-attention_emcr_cat_recall_by_early_lpp layout-ncol="2"}
![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_lpp_by_recall_NEGATIVE_EARLYLPP.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_lpp_by_recall_NEUTRAL_EARLYLPP.png)

Early LPP by recall status for NEGATIVE items (LEFT) and NEUTRAL items (RIGHT) under Pure-Attention eCMR.
:::

### Notes
While this text focus on just one specification, we considered two variants of pure-attention eCMR: one where emotional-attention scales context-to-item associations multiplicatively with primacy-based attention, and one where the two forms of attention combine additively.
Preliminary simulations suggested that the additive variant better captured the emotion-enhanced memory effect without over-predicting recall rates for negative items in early study positions.

But both variants struggled to capture the emotion-enhanced memory effect adequately, both underestimating recall rates for negative items and overestimating recall rates for neutral items.
The deficiency was biggest for negative items in later study positions, and for neutral items in study positions up through position 8. 

The data exhibits considerable variability in recall rates for both item types across positions, and the model is maybe only able to capture the general trend beyond boosts in early and late positions characteristic of a U-shaped serial position effect.

Crucially, the pure-attention eCMR variant does not incorporate any mechanisms to capture the relationship between LPP amplitude and recall probability within each item category.

## Simplified Two-Context eCMR

Another approach to simplifying eCMR is to retain its two-context structure but pare down the number of free parameters and mechanisms.
In this approach, we retain the emotional and temporal contexts, but make the key simplification of fixing the integration rate of emotional context to 1.0.
This means that the emotional context at each step of either encoding or retrieval is determined entirely by the most recently processed item, making emotional feature-to-context memory (eMFC) effectively redundant since emotional context can be updated directly from item features.

The emotional context-to-feature memory (eMCF) is retained and represented as a vector where cells corresponding to emotional items hold a value scaled by a `emotion_scale` parameter, while cells corresponding to neutral items hold a value of 0.

An additional parameter sometimes used in eCMR to scale the relative contribution of temporal vs emotional context during retrieval is redundant under this specification, since the `emotion_scale` parameter already modulates that balance. With specialized drift rates and feature-to-context memory learning rates also excluded, this simplified two-layer eCMR addresses the emotion-enhanced memory effect with still just one additional free parameter beyond those specified in baseline CMR.

Whereas the pure-attention eCMR variant postulates that emotional-attention modulates context-to-item associations directly, the two-context eCMR variant predicts that the boost in recall for emotional items arises from their ability to reinstate emotional context, which then preferentially cues other emotional items. We can't directly evaluate this mechanism with the present dataset since we lack recall order data. Nonetheless, the model may still make different predictions about overall recall rates for emotional and neutral items than the pure-attention variant and at minimum can be evaluated for its ability to capture the emotion-enhanced memory effect.

### Parameter Sensitivity Tests

::: {#fig-two_context_emcr_parameter_sensitivity layout-ncol="2"}
![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AdditiveIsolatedArousalSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_negative_shift_emotion_scale.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AdditiveIsolatedArousalSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_neutral_shift_emotion_scale.png)

Sensitivity of serial position curve to changes in the emotional-attention scaling parameter in Two-Context eCMR. Left: NEGATIVE items. Right: NEUTRAL items.
:::

::: {#fig-attention_emcr_parameter_sensitivity layout-ncol="2"}

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_negative_shift_emotion_scale.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_neutral_shift_emotion_scale.png)

**Reproduced for comparison**: Sensitivity of serial position curve to changes in the emotional-attention scaling parameter in pure-attention eCMR. Left: NEGATIVE items. Right: NEUTRAL items.
:::

Compared to pure-attention eCMR, the two-context eCMR variant shows a similar sensitivity to changes in the emotional-attention scaling parameter, with increases in the parameter boosting recall rates for negative items while reducing recall rates for neutral items.
The Two-Context eCMR variant appears to produce a bigger boost to recall rates for the first and last study positions for negative items, while the pure-attention variant seems to apply some kind of soft ceiling on recall rates for negative items in the first and last study positions.

### Comparison of Fits to Benchmark Statistics

#### Negative + Neutral Category SPC

::: {#fig-two_context_vs_attention_cat_spc layout-ncol="2"}
![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AdditiveIsolatedArousalSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc.png)

Category-SPC for NEGATIVE and NEUTRAL items combined. Left column: Two-Context eCMR. Right column: Pure-Attention eCMR.
:::

Both variants capture the broad U-shaped trend, though the two-context model produces a slightly sharper emotional advantage late in the list.

#### Negative-Category SPC

::: {#fig-two_context_vs_attention_cat_spc_negative layout-ncol="2"}
![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AdditiveIsolatedArousalSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_negative.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_negative.png)

Category-SPC for NEGATIVE items. Left column: Two-Context eCMR. Right column: Pure-Attention eCMR.
:::

#### Neutral-Category SPC

::: {#fig-two_context_vs_attention_cat_spc_neutral layout-ncol="2"}
![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AdditiveIsolatedArousalSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_neutral.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_neutral.png)

Category-SPC for NEUTRAL items. Left column: Two-Context eCMR. Right column: Pure-Attention eCMR.
:::

#### Regular Serial Position Curve

::: {#fig-two_context_vs_attention_spc layout-ncol="2"}
![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AdditiveIsolatedArousalSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_spc.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_spc.png)

Overall serial position curve across item types. Left column: Two-Context eCMR. Right column: Pure-Attention eCMR.
:::

#### Notes
The Two-Context eCMR variant seems to produce a slightly better fit to the emotion-enhanced memory effect than the Pure-Attention variant, but only if you squint your eyes a bit; broad issues with under-prediction of recall rates for negative items and over-prediction for neutral items remain.

For now, we use both variants as candidates for neurally-informed extensions of eCMR.

## Neurally Informed Extension

We now turn to exploring how a neural signature known to carry information about whether negative items will be recalled or not can further augment model performance.
Our focus for the moment is an initial prototype that efficiently demonstrates that such improvement using present data is *possible*.
Downstream work can attempt to identify the *best* approach.

To extend the Pure-Attention simplified eCMR model, we let Early LPP perturb each emotional item’s learning rate.
For every trial we center Early LPP within emotional study events, zeroing neutral items and measuring only the deviation from the emotional mean.
During encoding this deviation is scaled by `lpp_scale` and added to the primacy-driven learning rate.
The sum is clamped at zero so low-LPP emotional items never become stronger than neutral items, while high-LPP items enjoy proportionally higher associations.
This additive term is the only change relative to the Pure-Attention eCMR model described above, giving the global `emotion_scale` the same role as before and using LPP to redistribute recall probability across emotional list items.
Here, the goal is to exploit the relationship between EarlyLPP and recall probability for emotional items observed in the data, allowing the model to reduce support in the model for emotional items that were not recalled in the data, while boosting support for emotional items that were recalled.

A similar approach can be taken to extend the Two-Context simplified eCMR model.
Here, Early LPP again perturbs each emotional item’s learning rate during encoding but only for the emotional context-to-item associations stored in eMCF.
Modulation is scaled by `lpp_scale`, and the sum is again clamped at zero.
This change specifically allows the model to redistribute the ability of emotional items to be cued for by emotional context during retrieval, based on their Early LPP values.

### Parameter Sensitivity Tests

Both neurally-informed eCMR variants introduce a new `lpp_scale` parameter that scales the influence of Early LPP on emotional item learning rates during encoding.
To confirm that this parameter can help the models capture the relationship between LPP amplitude and recall probability, we simulate each model variant across a range of `lpp_scale` values, plotting the emotional category-spc and Early LPP by recall status for negative items for each configuration.
Both variants seem to make similar use of the new parameter to adjust recall rates for emotional items based on their LPP values.

#### Neurally-Informed Pure-Attention eCMR

::: {#fig-eeg_attention_emcr_cat_spc_parameter_sensitivity layout-ncol="2"}

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_EEGAttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_negative_shift_lpp_scale.png)

![](/results/figures/TalmiEEG_EEGAttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_neutral_shift_lpp_scale.png)

Sensitivity of serial position curve to changes in the LPP scaling parameter in Neurally-Informed eCMR. Left: NEGATIVE items. Right: NEUTRAL items.
:::

::: {#fig-eeg_attention_emcr_negative_lpp_by_recall layout-ncol="2"}
![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_EEGAttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_lpp_by_recall_NEGATIVE_UNRECALLED_shift_lpp_scale.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_EEGAttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_lpp_by_recall_NEGATIVE_RECALLED_shift_lpp_scale.png)

Sensitivity of Early LPP by recall status for NEGATIVE items to changes in the LPP scaling parameter in Neurally-Informed eCMR. Left: UNRECALLED items. Right: RECALLED items.
:::

#### Neurally-Informed Two-Context eCMR

::: {#fig-eeg_attention_emcr_cat_spc_parameter_sensitivity layout-ncol="2"}

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AdditiveIsolatedArousalSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_negative_shift_lpp_scale.png)

![](/results/figures/TalmiEEG_AdditiveIsolatedArousalSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_neutral_shift_lpp_scale.png)

Sensitivity of serial position curve to changes in the LPP scaling parameter in Neurally-Informed eCMR. Left: NEGATIVE items. Right: NEUTRAL items.
:::

::: {#fig-eeg_attention_emcr_negative_lpp_by_recall layout-ncol="2"}
![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AdditiveIsolatedArousalSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_lpp_by_recall_NEGATIVE_UNRECALLED_shift_lpp_scale.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AdditiveIsolatedArousalSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_lpp_by_recall_NEGATIVE_RECALLED_shift_lpp_scale.png)

Sensitivity of Early LPP by recall status for NEGATIVE items to changes in the LPP scaling parameter in Neurally-Informed eCMR. Left: UNRECALLED items. Right: RECALLED items.
:::

### Comparison of Fits to Benchmark Statistics

#### Negative + Neutral Category SPC

::: {#fig-eeg_attention_emcr_cat_spc_comparison}
![](results/figures/reference_catspc.png){width="50%"}

Recall rate by study position and item type from reference materials.
:::

::: {#fig-eegemcr_cat_spc layout-ncol="2"}

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_EEGAttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AdditiveIsolatedArousalSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_EEGTwoLayerSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc.png)

Recall rate by study position and item type for Regular/Neurally-Informed Pure-Attention eCMR (TOP LEFT/RIGHT), and Regular/Neurally-Informed Two-Context eCMR (BOTTOM LEFT/RIGHT).
:::

#### Negative-Category SPC

::: {#fig-eegemcr_cat_spc_negative layout-ncol="2"}

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_negative.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_EEGAttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_negative.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AdditiveIsolatedArousalSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_negative.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_EEGTwoLayerSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_negative.png)

Recall rate for NEGATIVE items by study position, for Regular/Neurally-Informed Pure-Attention eCMR (TOP LEFT/RIGHT), and Regular/Neurally-Informed Two-Context eCMR (BOTTOM LEFT/RIGHT).
:::

#### Neutral-Category SPC

::: {#fig-eegemcr_cat_spc_neutral layout-ncol="2"}

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_neutral.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_EEGAttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_neutral.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AdditiveIsolatedArousalSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_neutral.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_EEGTwoLayerSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc_neutral.png)

Recall rate for NEUTRAL items by study position, for Regular/Neurally-Informed Pure-Attention eCMR (TOP LEFT/RIGHT), and Regular/Neurally-Informed Two-Context eCMR (BOTTOM LEFT/RIGHT).
:::

#### Regular Serial Position Curve

::: {#fig-eegemcr_spc layout-ncol="2"}

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_spc.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_EEGAttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_spc.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AdditiveIsolatedArousalSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_spc.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_EEGTwoLayerSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_spc.png)

Recall rate for ALL items by study position, for Regular/Neurally-Informed Pure-Attention eCMR (TOP LEFT/RIGHT), and Regular/Neurally-Informed Two-Context eCMR (BOTTOM LEFT/RIGHT).
:::

#### Early LPP by Recall Status and Item-Category

::: {#fig-eegecmr_lpp_by_recall_reference layout-ncol="2"}

![](results/figures/cat_lpp_by_recall_EarlyLPP_Negative.png)

LPP amplitude by recall status for NEGATIVE items from reference materials.
:::

::: {#fig-eegecmr_lpp_by_recall layout-ncol="2"}

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_lpp_by_recall_NEGATIVE_EARLYLPP.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_EEGAttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_lpp_by_recall_NEGATIVE_EARLYLPP.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AdditiveIsolatedArousalSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_lpp_by_recall_NEGATIVE_EARLYLPP.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_EEGTwoLayerSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_lpp_by_recall_NEGATIVE_EARLYLPP.png)

Early LPP by recall status for NEGATIVE items, for Regular/Neurally-Informed Pure-Attention eCMR (TOP LEFT/RIGHT), and Regular/Neurally-Informed Two-Context eCMR (BOTTOM LEFT/RIGHT).
:::

### Notes

# Side Issues

## Do arousal and serial position additively or interactively influence memory strength?

The specification for eCMR seems to configure a multiplicative relationship between emotional-attention and primacy-attention.
I think that leads to the prediction that the recall rate gap for emotional vs neutral items depends on the compared items' study positions: higher for items studied near the beginning of a list than elsewhere.

Here's a worked example:

If the primacy-attention value at the first study position is 10 and the list-wide emotional attention scale is 3, then the total attentional boost at that study position is 10\*3=30 for emotional items but just 10 for neutral items.
So the difference in attention for emotional vs neutral items is 20.

At a later study position, the primacy attention value falls to 1 and the emotional-attention scale is still 3.
This makes the total attentional boost at that study position 1\*3=3 for emotional items but just 1 for neutral items.
The difference in attention for emotional vs neutral items is just 2.

That's ten times lower than the emotional vs neutral boost at study position 1!
So according to this specification, the attentional boost that drives the emotion-enhanced memory effect is stronger for early study positions than late study positions.

The present data and model evaluation pipeline present only a limited opportunity to examine whether emotion and primacy independently/additively or interactively/multiplicatively configure emotional context-to-item memory strengths.
Here we draw separate plots for data and each model variant.

::: {#fig-additive_vs_multiplicative_cat_spc layout-ncol="3"}

![](results/figures/reference_catspc.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_AttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc.png)

![](results/figures/TalmiEEG_MultiplicativeAttentionSimpleECMRNoStop_cat_spc_mse_fixed_term_best_of_3_cat_spc.png)

Recall rate by study position and item type for data (LEFT), Additive eCMR simulation (MIDDLE), and Multiplicative eCMR simulation (RIGHT).
:::

These simulations suggest that primacy and emotionality *independently* rather than interactively modulate memory strength.
The gap between the two variants seems localized to their predictions about recall rates for negative items in early study positions.
The multiplicative model tends to predict a much higher recall rate for these items than observed in the data, while the additive model produces no such discrepancy.
We thus use the additive model for simulations going forward, and propose pursuing a paper focused on this distinction as a future project.

## Improving Trade-Off Between Recency and Primacy

Baseline and eCMR fits seem to give low recall rates to the last few study positions in our serial position curves.
One possible reason is that CMR lacks a mechanism to eliminate recency effects without boosting the strength of the primacy effect (through start-of-list context reinstatement).
Since the data exhibits neither a strong recency nor a strong primacy effect, the model struggles to capture it.

On the other hand, it could be that eCMR's complete specification is necessary to avoid this down-modulation of the serial position effect.
However, since eCMR's eMCF only affects recall rates for emotional items, I think it unlikely to help address the down-modulation observed for the neutral-SPC.

A common approach in retrieved-context models to attenuate the recency effect is to introduce a delay drift rate following the study phase, causing context to drift toward an out-of-list context before retrieval begins.
The rationale is that by shifting the contextual cue away from its final study state, recently studied items will receive less contextual overlap and hence less retrieval advantage.

However, this mechanism generally fails to balance primacy and recency because it does not alter the relative similarity between the contextual cue and the study-phase contexts of individual items.
Context moves equally far from all studied items, preserving their relative strengths as retrieval cues.

To overcome this limitation, I tested a modified approach where post-study context drifts not toward an external or out-of-list context, but toward all in-list contexts, where the input vector is essentially a ones mask over context units that are linked to any studied item.
This adjustment removes temporal directionality from the final cue, effectively making recall initiation reflect just the differences in relative strength across context-to-item associations.

This didn't help either, so I'm leaving this problem for later..
